era_context: The year Theodosius I became Eastern Roman Emperor following the death of Valens at Adrianople (378 CE). The Eastern Empire was in crisis: the Goths had defeated and killed the emperor, and Germanic peoples were migrating into Roman territory. The Western Empire under Gratian was attempting to stabilize the situation. Christianity was rapidly expanding, though religious schisms (Arianism, Apollinarianism) remained contentious. This year marks the beginning of Theodosius's reign, which would reshape the late Roman Empire.

events.title: Theodosius I proclaimed Eastern Roman Emperor
events.description: Following the death of Valens at the Battle of Adrianople in August 378 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire was without an emperor. Gratian, the Western Roman Emperor, appointed Theodosius I (a Spanish-born general with military experience in Britain and the Balkans) as Eastern Roman Emperor in January 379 CE. Theodosius was proclaimed in Sirmium (modern Serbia) and immediately faced the task of stabilizing the empire after the catastrophic defeat and the ongoing Gothic incursions. His appointment marked a turning point: Theodosius would prove to be one of the most significant emperors of the late Roman period, eventually reunifying the empire and establishing Christianity as the state religion.
key_figures: Theodosius I (newly proclaimed Eastern Roman Emperor)
Gratian (Western Roman Emperor, who appointed Theodosius)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers the succession)

events.certainty_note: The appointment of Theodosius in January 379 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. The date and location are secure.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE)
Part of broader Theodosius I reign (379–395 CE)

events.title: Gothic settlement negotiations and military campaigns
events.description: In 379 CE, Theodosius I initiated military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations with the Goths and other barbarian groups that had invaded the Eastern Empire following Adrianople. Rather than attempting immediate reconquest, Theodosius pursued a strategy of containment and negotiation. These efforts would eventually lead to the foederati treaty of 382 CE, in which Goths were settled within Roman territory as allied troops. The 379 CE campaigns represent the beginning of this pragmatic approach, which contrasted with earlier Roman military doctrine and reflected the empire's weakened military capacity.
key_figures: Alaric (Gothic leader, though his prominence increased later)

sources.name: Jordanes, Getica (written 6th century, covers Gothic history)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's military activities in 379 CE are attested in later sources, but specific details are sparse. The broader strategy of negotiation is secure; exact chronology of individual campaigns is less certain.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Religious tensions and Arian-Nicene disputes in the Eastern Empire
events.description: In 379 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire was deeply divided over Christian theology. The Arian controversy (which had begun in the early 4th century) remained unresolved despite the Council of Nicaea (325 CE). Many Eastern bishops and even some emperors had favored Arianism or semi-Arian positions. Theodosius I, though a Christian, initially inherited an empire where religious orthodoxy was contested. His appointment as emperor would eventually lead to his enforcement of Nicene orthodoxy via the Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE), but in 379 CE, the religious landscape was still fluid and contested.
key_figures: Theodosius I (newly proclaimed emperor, Christian but not yet enforcing orthodoxy)
Gregory of Nazianzus (Nicene theologian, would become influential)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations (written late 4th century, discusses religious tensions)
Historiographic consensus on late 4th-century Christian disputes

events.certainty_note: The existence of religious tensions in 379 CE is well-attested; specific events or developments within this year are less clearly documented.
cross_references: Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE)
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Theodosius as a strong, decisive emperor who immediately stabilized the empire is somewhat idealized in later sources. Modern scholarship (e.g., Peter Brown, Ramsay MacMullen) suggests that Theodosius's early years were marked by significant challenges, military setbacks, and a gradual consolidation of power rather than immediate success. The foederati system, often portrayed as a pragmatic solution, may have been more of a necessity born from military weakness than a deliberate strategic choice.
historiographic_note: The year 379 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding Theodosius's appointment and the broader political situation. However, sources are predominantly written from the perspective of the imperial court and the Nicene Christian establishment. Information on barbarian perspectives, non-Nicene Christian communities, and the experiences of ordinary people is limited. Military details are sparse compared to political narratives.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's appointment as emperor in 379 CE led to his issuance of the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE.
Military campaigns and negotiations in 379 CE led to the foederati treaty with the Goths in 382 CE.
Theodosius's appointment was a direct consequence of the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE.
